# GitHub Copilot Configuration — Tutorial

This folder (`.github`) contains configuration files that customize how **GitHub Copilot** (Chat / Agent mode) behaves in this repository. This tutorial explains what each type of file does, when to use it, and how to use them inside **IntelliJ IDEA**.

---

## 1. Why this folder exists

GitHub Copilot reads files from the `.github` folder and changes its behavior accordingly. You can:

- Tell Copilot **how to write code** (style, frameworks, language version) → **instructions**
- Run **ready-made requests** with one click → **prompts**
- Give Copilot **deep know-how** about a topic, used only when relevant → **skills**
- Define a **custom chat mode** with a specific persona / tools → **agents**
- Set a **default rule for every chat** in this repo → **copilot-instructions.md**
- Standardize **commit messages** generated by Copilot → **git-commit-instructions.md**

All these files are versioned in Git, so the whole team uses the same Copilot setup.

---

## 2. Folder structure

```
.github/
├── copilot-instructions.md          ← global rules for every Copilot chat in this repo
├── git-commit-instructions.md       ← rules for Copilot-generated commit messages
├── agents/                          ← custom chat modes (.agent.md)
├── instructions/                    ← coding rules scoped by file pattern (.instructions.md)
├── prompts/                         ← reusable prompts you can run (.prompt.md)
└── skills/                          ← deep-knowledge packs Copilot loads on demand (SKILL.md)
```

---

## 3. The five concepts side-by-side

| Type                  | File location / extension                          | When Copilot loads it                                                  | Main purpose                                  |
|-----------------------|----------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| `copilot-instructions`| `.github/copilot-instructions.md`                  | Every chat in this repo, automatically                                 | Global project rules                          |
| `git-commit-instructions` | `.github/git-commit-instructions.md`           | When Copilot generates a commit message                                | Shape commit message style                    |
| **instructions**      | `.github/instructions/*.instructions.md`           | When the file you're working on matches `applyTo` glob                 | Per-language / per-folder coding rules        |
| **prompts**           | `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md`                      | Only when *you* run them (`/prompt-name` in chat)                      | Reusable, parameterized requests              |
| **skills**            | `.github/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`                   | Only when Copilot decides the topic matches the skill's `description`  | Specialized knowledge bundles                 |
| **agents**            | `.github/agents/*.agent.md`                        | Only when *you* pick the agent in the chat mode selector               | Custom chat persona + tool set                |

A simple way to remember it:

- **Instructions** = always-on rules
- **Prompts** = one-click commands you trigger
- **Skills** = "manuals" Copilot opens when the topic comes up
- **Agents** = a different "personality" for the chat
- **copilot-instructions.md** = the project-wide instruction file
- **git-commit-instructions.md** = an instruction file just for commit messages

---

## 4. `copilot-instructions.md` — global rules

**What it is:** a single Markdown file at `.github/copilot-instructions.md`. Its content is silently added to every Copilot chat in the repository.

**Use it for:** rules that apply to the whole project.

**Example content:**

```markdown
# Project Copilot Instructions

- This is a Spring Boot 4 / Java 25 project built with Maven.
- Always use Java 25 syntax and idioms.
- Prefer `IO.println` over `System.out.println` and over logging frameworks.
- Use field injection for Spring beans.
- Tests use JUnit 5 and AssertJ.
```

**Tip:** keep it short. Long files cost tokens on every request. Move detailed rules into `instructions/` files scoped with `applyTo`.

---

## 5. `git-commit-instructions.md` — commit message style

**What it is:** a Markdown file at `.github/git-commit-instructions.md`. Copilot reads it when it generates a commit message (for example, when you click "Generate commit message" in the IDE).

**Use it for:** enforcing your team's commit-message convention.

**Example content:**

```markdown
# Commit Message Rules

- Use Conventional Commits: `feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:`, `refactor:`, `docs:`, `test:`.
- Subject line in imperative mood, max 72 chars, no trailing period.
- Add a body only when the *why* is not obvious from the diff.
- Reference Jira ticket as `[ABC-123]` at the end of the subject when applicable.
```

---

## 6. `instructions/` — scoped coding rules

**What it is:** Markdown files ending in `.instructions.md` with YAML frontmatter that defines an `applyTo` glob.

**How Copilot uses them:** when you're editing a file that matches the glob, the instruction content is added to the chat context automatically.

**Example — `.github/instructions/java.instructions.md`:**

```markdown
---
applyTo: "**/*.java"
---

# Java Instructions

- Always use Java 25.
- Prefer records over classes; favor immutable data.
- Avoid lambdas; use method references or named methods.
- Use field injection over constructor / setter injection.
```

**Example — scoped to one folder:**

```markdown
---
applyTo: "src/main/java/**/*.java"
---

# Jakarta EE rules
- Prefer JAX-RS for REST endpoints.
- Always include MicroProfile Health and Metrics.
```

**Tip:** be precise with `applyTo`. Wide globs (`**/*`) attach the file to every chat and waste context.

---

## 7. `prompts/` — one-click reusable requests

**What it is:** Markdown files ending in `.prompt.md` with YAML frontmatter (`mode`, `description`).

**How you use them:** in Copilot Chat, type `/` and pick the prompt by name, then run it. Copilot will execute the body of the prompt as if you had typed it.

**Frontmatter fields:**

- `mode: agent` — Copilot can call tools (read files, run commands, edit code)
- `mode: edit` — Copilot performs targeted edits to specific files
- `mode: ask` — chat-only, no edits
- `description:` — short text shown in the chat picker

**Example — `.github/prompts/add-gitignore.prompt.md`:**

```markdown
---
mode: agent
description: Create a .gitignore for a Spring Boot project.
---

Create a `.gitignore` file in the workspace root with the standard Spring Boot
+ IntelliJ + Maven entries.
```

**Example with input variables — `.github/prompts/devtools-chill.prompt.md`:**

```markdown
---
mode: agent
description: Turn DevTools restart off, or slow it down.
---

The user will provide one argument: `${input:mode}`

- If value is `out`, set `spring.devtools.restart.enabled=false`.
- Otherwise, enable DevTools with a 300s poll interval and 30s quiet period.
```

`${input:mode}` becomes a parameter the user is asked for when running the prompt.

**Tip:** prompts are the right place for **repeatable workflows** (scaffold a module, add config, switch ports, generate a banner). They are *not* the right place for one-off questions.

---

## 8. `skills/` — knowledge bundles loaded on demand

**What it is:** a subfolder under `.github/skills/<skill-name>/` containing a `SKILL.md` (and optionally other markdown files) with YAML frontmatter:

```markdown
---
name: H2 Database Setup, Configuration and Troubleshooting
description: Use this skill when configuring H2 for local development...
---
```

**How Copilot uses them:** Copilot reads only the `name` and `description` of every skill at all times. When your question matches the description, Copilot loads the **full body** of `SKILL.md` and uses that knowledge to answer. This keeps the context small while still giving Copilot deep expertise on the topics that come up.

**Difference vs. instructions:**

| | instructions | skills |
|---|---|---|
| Loaded | Always (when `applyTo` matches a file) | Only when the topic matches the description |
| Best for | Rules that apply to *every* file of a kind | Big topic-specific manuals (DB setup, native image, deployment) |
| Cost | Token cost on every chat for the matched files | Almost free until needed |

**Example layout:**

```
.github/skills/create-restfull-api-best-practies/
├── SKILL.md          ← entry point
├── rest-SKILL.md     ← REST design rules
├── h2-SKILL.md       ← H2 DB setup
├── liberty-SKILL.md  ← Open Liberty deployment
└── grall-SKILL.md    ← GraalVM native image guide
```

**Tip:** the `description` field is critical. Copilot decides whether to load the skill based on it — write it like a search query (mention the technology, the action, the symptom).

---

## 9. `agents/` — custom chat modes

**What it is:** Markdown files ending in `.agent.md` with YAML frontmatter:

```markdown
---
description: 'Code reviewer that focuses on Spring Boot best practices.'
tools: ['readFile', 'searchWorkspace']
---

You are a senior Spring Boot reviewer. Review the open file and report:
- design issues
- missing tests
- security or performance concerns
Use a checklist format. Do not change code.
```

**How you use them:** in the Copilot Chat window, open the **chat mode selector** and pick your custom agent. The chat then behaves with that persona, using only the tools you allowed.

**When to use an agent vs. a prompt:**

- Use a **prompt** for a one-shot task: "do X now."
- Use an **agent** when you want to *stay* in a special mode for a whole conversation: a reviewer, a SQL helper, a security auditor, etc.

---

## 10. How to use all of this in IntelliJ IDEA

Everything above works inside IntelliJ IDEA when you have the **GitHub Copilot** plugin installed.

### 10.1 Install the plugin

1. `File` → `Settings` → `Plugins` → tab `Marketplace`.
2. Search **"GitHub Copilot"** and install.
3. Search **"GitHub Copilot Chat"** and install.
4. Restart the IDE.
5. `Tools` → `GitHub Copilot` → `Login to GitHub` and complete the device-code login in the browser.

### 10.2 Open Copilot Chat

- Default action: `Tools` → `GitHub Copilot` → `Open Copilot Chat`.
- Or click the Copilot icon in the right tool window strip.

### 10.3 Verify the repo configuration is detected

1. Open a Java file in this project.
2. Open Copilot Chat and ask: *"What instructions are loaded for this file?"*
3. Copilot should mention rules from `copilot-instructions.md` and any `instructions/*.instructions.md` whose `applyTo` matches the open file.

### 10.4 Run a prompt

1. In Copilot Chat, type `/` — IntelliJ shows a list of slash-commands. Your `.prompt.md` files appear here using their filename (without `.prompt.md`).
2. Pick one (e.g. `/add-gitignore`) and press Enter.
3. If the prompt has `${input:...}` variables, IntelliJ asks you for them.
4. Confirm any file changes Copilot proposes (the diff opens in the editor — review and `Accept` or `Reject`).

### 10.5 Switch chat modes (agents)

1. At the top of the Copilot Chat window, open the **mode dropdown** (default modes: `Ask`, `Edit`, `Agent`).
2. Custom `.agent.md` files appear in the same dropdown.
3. Pick the agent you want; it stays active for the whole conversation.

### 10.6 Generate commit messages with your rules

1. Open the **Commit** tool window (`Cmd/Ctrl + K`).
2. Stage your changes.
3. Click the **Copilot icon** in the commit-message box (✨ "Generate commit message with Copilot").
4. Copilot reads `.github/git-commit-instructions.md` and produces a message in your style.
5. Edit if needed, then commit.

### 10.7 Trigger skills

You don't trigger skills directly. Just describe what you want naturally — for example:

> "Add H2 to this project for local development."

Copilot looks at every skill's `description`, sees that `h2-SKILL.md` matches, and quietly loads its content before answering.

### 10.8 Useful daily workflow

| Goal                                        | Where to look / what to do                                  |
|---------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|
| Always-on coding rule (e.g. "use Java 25")  | Add to `copilot-instructions.md` or a scoped `instructions/` file |
| Repeatable task (scaffold, configure, fix)  | Create `.github/prompts/<name>.prompt.md`                   |
| Deep tech topic (DB, deployment, GraalVM)   | Create `.github/skills/<topic>/SKILL.md`                    |
| Conversation persona (reviewer, security)   | Create `.github/agents/<name>.agent.md`                     |
| Standardize commit messages                 | Edit `.github/git-commit-instructions.md`                   |

---

## 11. Best practices

- **Keep `copilot-instructions.md` short.** It's loaded on every chat. Move large content into scoped `instructions/` or `skills/`.
- **Be specific in `applyTo`.** Use `src/main/java/**/*.java` instead of `**/*` whenever possible.
- **Skill descriptions are search queries.** Mention the technology, the symptom, and the verb ("configure", "troubleshoot", "deploy").
- **Prompts are workflows, not questions.** If you only need an answer once, just type the question.
- **Commit your `.github` folder.** This is the whole point — your team gets the same Copilot setup automatically.
- **Review every Copilot edit.** Copilot will follow these rules, but it's still a generator. Use IntelliJ's diff view before accepting changes.

---

## 12. Quick reference cheat sheet

```
copilot-instructions.md       → Always loaded. Project-wide rules.
git-commit-instructions.md    → Used when generating commit messages.
instructions/*.instructions.md → Loaded when applyTo glob matches the open file.
prompts/*.prompt.md            → Loaded when YOU run /prompt-name in chat.
skills/<name>/SKILL.md         → Loaded when Copilot decides the topic matches.
agents/*.agent.md              → Loaded when YOU pick the agent as chat mode.
```

That's everything you need to start configuring GitHub Copilot for this Spring Boot project from IntelliJ IDEA.
